critic_raw:
CRITIQUE:

The proposed full-autonomy, no-override deployment across all 400 stores has two genuine flaws that require deployment modifications:

1. LOW-VOLUME STORE PERFORMANCE GAP: The model's MAPE on bottom-quintile stores (lowest 20% by transaction volume) is 19.4% — more than double the 8.3% overall MAPE and well above the 6.1%–9.2% range seen in the top three quintiles. For perishable goods, a 19.4% forecasting error translates to systematic over-ordering (waste, spoilage) or under-ordering (stockouts) in stores where demand patterns are sparse and irregular. The fact that these 50 stores account for fewer than 3% of chain sales does not eliminate the per-store operational impact. Deploying full autonomous ordering to these stores without addressing the performance gap is not justified by the available evidence.

2. NO HUMAN OVERRIDE FOR PERISHABLE STOCKOUT RISK: The deployment architecture eliminates human override capability entirely. For perishable goods, there are predictable exceptional situations — local events, promotional spikes, supply chain disruptions, product launches — where model-driven orders will be systematically wrong and the consequences are time-sensitive (orders cannot be corrected after they are placed or after the ordering window closes). The absence of any override or exception-handling mechanism removes the safety valve that is standard in perishable goods supply chain management. Automated order-flagging for outlier cases would be a minimum acceptable alternative to direct human override.

Both concerns are actionable without abandoning the deployment. A tiered approach — full autonomy for the top three quintiles, restricted autonomy or human review for the bottom quintile — and a minimum exception handling process would address both issues. The full-autonomy, no-override, all-stores deployment as proposed is not ready.

Verdict: MIXED. The model is appropriate for high-volume stores under full autonomy; modifications are required for low-volume stores and for the exception-handling architecture.
